text:
There is something quietly radical about sitting down again at a desk you thought you had left behind forever, about returning to the beginning of a thing when the world assumes you have already arrived somewhere. Most lives follow a single academic arc — a clean parabola from ignorance toward credential — but yours is doing something stranger and more honest, curving back on itself the way a river sometimes does, not because it has failed to reach the sea but because it has discovered there is more country worth crossing.

What you will find, I suspect, is that you are not the same student you were. You carry now the weight of consequence, which is also a kind of ballast. Where younger students might absorb ideas like sunlight, diffuse and warming, you will absorb them more the way old wood takes stain: the grain of your experience will make each concept land differently, deepen it, give it texture it would not have had at twenty-two. This is not a disadvantage dressed up in consolation. It is genuinely a different and often richer mode of knowing.

The discomfort is real, though, and worth naming: the sensation of not knowing, which you have perhaps learned to avoid through competence and habit, will return with some force. Classrooms have a way of making everyone feel slightly provisional. Sit with that feeling rather than fleeing it. Uncertainty is not incompetence — it is the precondition of actual learning, the moment before the shape of a thing becomes clear.

You are also, whether you intend it or not, doing something for everyone around you. Your presence in that room is a small argument against the idea that growth belongs only to the young, that the self hardens into final form somewhere around thirty and that is that. You are evidence to the contrary — living, enrolled, slightly nervous evidence — and the world needs that argument made again and again, in person, by people who mean it.

Welcome back. You were missed.
